title: Blocks, Not Just Layers
concept: Modern CNNs are usually designed as repeated blocks or modules — small groups of layers that work together — rather than as one giant unbroken list of single layers.

exercises.prompt: Recap: If the same small layer pattern appears again and again inside a CNN, what is the most helpful way to think about it?

options.text: As a reusable block or module
As unrelated layers that just happened to land next to each other
As proof the network is broken and repeating by mistake

exercises.explain: Yes — repeated layer groups are usually treated as blocks or modules, which makes a big architecture easier to understand.

cards.title: Architects see chunks
cards.body: When experts read a CNN diagram, they usually do not narrate every single layer one by one. They spot chunks: a repeated pattern here, a downsampling stage there, a classifier head at the end. That chunk-based view is how modern architectures are actually designed.

cards.title: What is a block?
cards.body: A block is a small group of layers that works together as one mini-module. For example, Conv -> ReLU -> Conv -> ReLU -> Pool can be treated as one feature-extraction block. CNNs often repeat a familiar block shape several times, changing only details like channel count.

cards.title: Rooms, not individual bricks
cards.body: If you tour a school building, you usually think in rooms and hallways, not in every brick and screw. A CNN is similar. The bricks still matter, but the useful design unit is often the room — the block — because that is the level where the structure starts to make sense.

cards.title: A toy network built from two blocks
cards.body: Imagine this toy CNN: Block A = Conv(16) -> ReLU -> Conv(16) -> ReLU -> MaxPool. Then Block B = Conv(32) -> ReLU -> Conv(32) -> ReLU -> MaxPool. Then a classifier head. The exact layers matter, but the big idea is that the network is built from two repeated feature blocks, not from a random 10-layer list.

cards.title: A block is not one universal magic shape
cards.body: There is no single 'official' CNN block. Different model families use different ones. The key idea is not that every block is identical — it is that designers group cooperating layers into named modules so they can reason about the network at a useful scale.

cards.title: Layer-by-layer vs. block-by-block thinking
cards.body: Both views describe the same CNN, but one is much easier to reason about.

right.text:
Block 1: feature extractor
Block 2: deeper feature extractor
Head: final classifier

exercises.prompt: Which part of this toy architecture is most naturally treated as one block: Conv -> ReLU -> Conv -> ReLU -> Pool -> Dense?

options.text: Just the very first convolution and nothing else
There are no meaningful groups inside a CNN

exercises.explain: Right — that repeated feature-extraction chunk is exactly the kind of group designers call a block.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Modern CNN architects often reuse the same module pattern multiple times across a network.
exercises.explain: True — repetition is common because a proven block can be stacked and scaled in a predictable way.

exercises.sentence: Thinking in blocks helps you see the network as reusable ___ instead of one giant unbroken list of layers.
exercises.explain: Modules — that is exactly why the block idea is so useful in architecture design.

exercises.prompt: Why is block-based thinking so common in CNN design?

options.text: Because it makes repeated patterns easier to name, reuse, and understand
Because individual layers stop existing once a model gets deep
Because blocks are only about file organization and not about model design

exercises.explain: Exactly — blocks let you reason about a big architecture at the level of useful repeated parts.

exercises.prompt: A CNN diagram shows three repeated chunks, each shaped like Conv -> ReLU -> Conv -> ReLU -> Pool, followed by a dense classifier. What is the best way to describe that architecture?

options.text: It has three feature-extraction blocks followed by a classifier head
It is really just one layer, because repeated layers cancel each other out
It should only be read as a flat list of unrelated operations with no larger structure
It has no blocks because blocks are only used in language models, not CNNs

exercises.explain: Exactly. The layers are real, but the useful architectural description is three repeated blocks plus a head. Next we'll look at another common pattern inside those blocks: spatial size usually shrinks while channel depth grows.
